hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: Chapter II of this Constitution; Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy
content.plain_english: This chapter outlines the core principles and goals that should guide the Nigerian government in its actions and policies. These are not just aspirations but fundamental directives for the state.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Part IV: Directive Principles of State Policy (Articles 36-51)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Similar to Nigeria's Chapter II, India's Directive Principles are non-justiciable but fundamental in the governance of the country, guiding the state in making laws.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 45: Directive Principles of Social Policy
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The Irish Constitution also includes Directive Principles that are intended for the general guidance of the Oireachtas (parliament) but are not cognisable by any court.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (d) shall not, as from the date when this section comes into force, extend to any action or proceedings relating to any existing law made on or after 15th January, 1966 for determining any issue or question as to the competence of any authority or person to make any such law.
content.plain_english: This provision specifies that, from the date this particular section becomes active, it will not apply to any legal actions or cases concerning existing laws created on or after January 15, 1966, especially those questioning whether an authority or individual had the legal power to make such a law.

case_laws.case_title: Lakanmi and Anor v. Attorney-General (Western State) and Ors
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case dealt with the validity of decrees made by military governments and the ouster clauses attempting to prevent judicial review. While specific to a different era, it highlights the tension between legislative competence and judicial oversight, relevant to the 'competence of any authority' clause.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of the Federation v. Guardian Newspapers Ltd.
case_laws.relevance_summary: Though a later case, it touches on the limits of legislative power and the enforceability of laws, which can be indirectly related to questions of 'competence' to make laws.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: While different in nature, the concept of determining the 'competence' of an authority to make law is central to federal systems, where courts often rule on whether a state or federal body has acted within its constitutional powers (e.g., 'ultra vires' doctrine).

In the UK, the competence of Parliament to make any law is generally unquestioned by courts, but the concept of 'ultra vires' applies to delegated legislation, where courts can determine if a body acted beyond its statutory powers.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 7. (1) The system of local government by democratically elected local government councils is under this Constitution guaranteed; and accordingly, the Government of every State shall, subject to section 8 of this Constitution, ensure their existence under a Law which provides for the establishment, structure, composition, finance and functions of such councils.
content.plain_english: The Constitution guarantees a system of local government run by councils chosen through democratic elections. Therefore, each State government must ensure these councils exist by enacting a law that defines how they are set up, their organizational structure, who makes them up, how they are funded, and what their responsibilities are. This is subject to the provisions of Section 8.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Abia State & Ors v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case dealt with the powers of the National Assembly and State Houses of Assembly regarding local government administration, particularly the creation of new local governments and the distribution of funds, directly relevant to the guarantee and structure of local government.

case_laws.case_title: Governor of Ekiti State v. Olubunmo
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases involving the dissolution of local government councils or the appointment of caretaker committees often test the constitutional guarantee of democratically elected local governments.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The Indian Constitution explicitly guarantees and provides for the establishment, powers, and responsibilities of Panchayats (rural local self-governments) and Municipalities (urban local self-governments) through constitutional amendments, similar to Nigeria's guarantee.
South Africa's Constitution also guarantees the existence of local government, defining its status, objects, and powers, emphasizing democratic and accountable local governance.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (2) The person authorised by law to prescribe the area over which a local government council may exercise authority shall- (a) define such area as clearly as practicable; and (b) ensure, to the extent to which it may be reasonably justifiable that in defining such area regard is paid to - (i) the common interest of the community in the area; (ii) traditional association of the community; and (iii) administrative convenience.
content.plain_english: Whoever is legally empowered to define the geographical boundaries for a local government council must: (a) make these boundaries as clear as possible; and (b) ensure that when drawing these boundaries, they consider: (i) the shared interests of the people living in that area; (ii) the historical and cultural connections among the communities; and (iii) what makes the most practical sense for efficient administration.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Lagos State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, while primarily about the creation of new local governments, implicitly touches on the principles of defining local government areas and the legal authority to do so.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: State and Local Government powers (Tenth Amendment)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: While the US Constitution doesn't explicitly detail local government boundaries, state laws often include criteria for defining municipal boundaries, considering factors like community interest and administrative efficiency, often subject to judicial review for fairness and rationality.

Canadian provinces have exclusive jurisdiction over municipalities, and provincial legislation dictates how municipal boundaries are established and altered, often involving public consultations and consideration of community identity and service delivery.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (3) it shall be the duty of a local government council within the State to participate in economic planning and development of the area referred to in subsection (2) of this section and to this end an economic planning board shall be established by a Law enacted by the House of Assembly of the State.
content.plain_english: Local government councils in each State are responsible for helping with the economic planning and development of their designated areas (as defined in subsection 2). To achieve this, the State's House of Assembly must pass a law to create an economic planning board.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of the Federation v. Attorney-General of Lagos State
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, while about revenue allocation, highlights the roles and responsibilities of different tiers of government in economic matters, including local government's role in development.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: German municipalities have a constitutional right to manage their own affairs within the limits of the law, which includes local economic development and planning, often through local development agencies.

UK local authorities have powers and duties related to promoting the economic, social, and environmental well-being of their areas, often involving strategic planning and partnerships with local businesses.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (4) The Government of a State shall ensure that every person who is entitled to vote or be voted for at an election to House of Assembly shall have the right to vote or be voted for at an election to a local government council.
content.plain_english: Each State government must guarantee that anyone eligible to vote or run for election in the State House of Assembly also has the right to vote or run for election in local government council elections.

case_laws.case_title: INEC v. Musa
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case affirmed the right of political parties to participate in elections, which is foundational to the right to vote and be voted for at all levels of government.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US ensures broad suffrage for citizens in federal, state, and local elections, prohibiting discrimination based on race, sex, or age (18+). The principle of equal access to the ballot box across different tiers of government is fundamental.

German citizens have the right to vote in municipal elections, and the principles of universal, direct, free, equal, and secret suffrage apply to local elections as they do to federal and state elections.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (5) The functions to be conferred by Law upon local government council shall include those set out in the Fourth Schedule to this Constitution.
content.plain_english: The responsibilities given to local government councils by law must include all the duties listed in the Fourth Schedule of this Constitution.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Lagos State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This landmark case clarified the legislative competence of federal and state governments regarding local government functions, particularly concerning physical planning and environmental matters, which are often listed in schedules.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The Indian Constitution includes detailed schedules (Eleventh and Twelfth) that list the specific functions and powers devolved to Panchayats and Municipalities, similar to Nigeria's Fourth Schedule.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Schedules 4 and 5 (Functional Areas of Concurrent and Exclusive National and Provincial Legislative Competence)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: South Africa's Constitution outlines functional areas for different spheres of government, including local government, ensuring clarity on their responsibilities, though not always in a dedicated 'local government functions' schedule.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (6) Subject to the provisions of this Constitution - (a) the National Assembly shall make provisions for statutory allocation of public revenue to local government councils in the Federation; and (b) the House of Assembly of a State shall make provisions for statutory allocation of public revenue to local government councils within the State.
content.plain_english: In line with the Constitution's provisions: (a) The National Assembly is responsible for creating laws that ensure a fixed, legal distribution of public money to all local government councils across the country; and (b) Each State's House of Assembly must create laws for the fixed, legal distribution of public money to local government councils within that specific State.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of the Federation v. Attorney-General of Abia State & Ors
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case extensively discussed the constitutional provisions for revenue allocation, including the roles of the National Assembly and State Houses of Assembly in allocating funds to local governments.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Lagos State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: Further clarified the powers of the federal and state governments regarding local government finances and the creation of new local government areas, which impacts revenue allocation.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 280 (Finance Commission) and Article 243I (State Finance Commission)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India has a constitutional mechanism (Finance Commission at the Union level and State Finance Commissions) to recommend the distribution of tax revenues between the Union and States, and between States and local bodies, ensuring statutory allocation.

Germany's Basic Law outlines a complex system of revenue sharing and financial equalization between the federal government, states (Länder), and municipalities, ensuring local governments have the financial resources to perform their duties.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 8. (1) An Act of the National Assembly for the purpose of creating a new State shall only be passed if- (a) a request, supported by at least two-thirds majority of members (representing the area demanding the creation of the new State) in each of the following, namely - (i) the Senate and the House of Representatives, (ii) the House of Assembly in respect of the area, and (iii) the local government councils in respect of the area, is received by the National Assembly; (b) a proposal for the creation of the State is thereafter approved in a referendum by at least two-thirds majority of the people of the area where the demand for creation of the State originated; (c) the result of the referendum is then approved by a simple majority of all the States of the Federation supported by a simple majority of members of the Houses of Assembly; and (d) the proposal is approved by a resolution passed by two-thirds majority of members of each House of the National Assembly.
content.plain_english: To create a new State, the National Assembly can only pass a law if: (a) The National Assembly receives a formal request supported by at least two-thirds of the members from the affected area in the Senate, House of Representatives, the State House of Assembly, and the local government councils. (b) The people in the area requesting the new State approve the proposal in a referendum with at least a two-thirds majority vote. (c) The outcome of this referendum is then approved by a simple majority of all the States in the country, with the support of a simple majority of members in their respective Houses of Assembly. (d) Finally, the proposal is approved by a resolution passed by a two-thirds majority of members in both the Senate and the House of Representatives.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of the Federation v. Attorney-General of Abia State & Ors
case_laws.relevance_summary: While not directly about state creation, this case highlights the complex interplay between federal and state powers, which would be central to any process of creating new states or adjusting boundaries.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article IV, Section 3, Clause 1 (Admission of New States)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Constitution allows for the admission of new states, but it requires the consent of the legislatures of the states concerned as well as Congress, reflecting a federal process for territorial changes, though without the detailed referendum requirements.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 3 (Formation of new States and alteration of areas, boundaries or names of existing States)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Constitution allows Parliament to form new states by separating territory from any state, or by uniting two or more states, or by uniting any territory to a part of any state. It requires a bill to be introduced only on the recommendation of the President, who must refer it to the state legislature for its views, but Parliament is not bound by those views.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (2) An Act of the National Assembly for the purpose of boundary adjustment of any existing State shall only be passed if- (a) a request for the boundary adjustment, supported by two-thirds majority of members (representing the area demanding and the area affected by the boundary adjustment) in each of the following, namely- (i) the Senate and the House of Representatives, (ii) the House of Assembly in respect of the area, and (iii) the local government councils in respect of the area. is received by the National Assembly; and (b) a proposal for the boundary adjustment is approved by - (i) a simple majority of members of each House of the National Assembly, and (ii) a simple majority of members of the House of Assembly in respect of the area concerned.
content.plain_english: For the National Assembly to pass a law adjusting the boundary of an existing State, two conditions must be met: (a) The National Assembly must receive a request for the boundary change, supported by a two-thirds majority of members from both the requesting area and the area affected by the change, in the Senate, House of Representatives, the relevant State House of Assembly, and the local government councils. (b) The proposed boundary adjustment must then be approved by a simple majority of members in both the Senate and the House of Representatives, and also by a simple majority of members in the State House of Assembly for the affected area.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Cross River State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case involved a boundary dispute between states and the role of federal authorities in resolving such disputes, highlighting the constitutional framework for boundary adjustments.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article IV, Section 3, Clause 1 (Admission of New States)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Constitution requires the consent of the legislatures of the states concerned for any new state to be formed within the jurisdiction of any other state, or for any state to be formed by the junction of two or more states, or parts of states, reflecting a similar principle of state consent for boundary changes.

Changes to provincial boundaries in Canada typically require an amendment to the Constitution, which involves a complex process requiring the consent of the affected provinces and the federal Parliament, underscoring the significance of such changes.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (3) A bill for a Law of a House of Assembly for the purpose of creating a new local government area shall only be passed if - (a) a request supported by at least two-thirds majority of members (representing the area demanding the creation of the new local government area) in each of the following, namely - (i) the House of Assembly in respect of the area, and (ii) the local government councils in respect of the area, is received by the House of Assembly; (b) a proposal for the creation of the local government area is thereafter approved in a referendum by at least two-thirds majority of the people of the local government area where the demand for the proposed local government area originated; (c) the result of the referendum is then approved by a simple majority of the members in each local government council in a majority of all the local government councils in the State; and (d) the result of the referendum is approved by a resolution passed by two-thirds majority of members of the House of Assembly.
content.plain_english: A State House of Assembly can only pass a bill to create a new local government area if: (a) The House of Assembly receives a request supported by at least two-thirds of the members from the affected area in both the State House of Assembly and the local government councils. (b) The people in the area requesting the new local government area approve the proposal in a referendum with at least a two-thirds majority vote. (c) The outcome of this referendum is then approved by a simple majority of members in each local government council, provided this approval comes from a majority of all local government councils in the State. (d) Finally, the outcome of the referendum is approved by a resolution passed by a two-thirds majority of members of the State House of Assembly.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Lagos State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case directly addressed the powers of state governments to create new local government areas and the constitutional requirements for such creation, including the role of referendums.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: While not as detailed on the creation process, India's constitution empowers states to make laws regarding the composition and territorial constituencies of local bodies, often involving local consultations and legislative processes.

South Africa's Constitution mandates national and provincial legislation to define the types of municipalities and criteria for their establishment, often involving public participation and provincial government decisions.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (4) A bill for a Law of House of Assembly for the purpose of boundary adjustment of any existing local government area shall only be passed if- (a) a request for the boundary adjustment is supported by two-thirds majority of members (representing the area demanding and the area affected by the boundary adjustment) in each of the following, namely - (i) the House of Assembly in respect of the area, and (ii) the local government council in respect of the area, is received by the House of Assembly; and (b) a proposal for the boundary adjustment is approved by a simple majority of members of the House of Assembly in respect of the area concerned.
content.plain_english: A State House of Assembly can only pass a bill to adjust the boundary of an existing local government area if: (a) The House of Assembly receives a request for the boundary change, supported by a two-thirds majority of members from both the requesting area and the area affected by the change, in the State House of Assembly and the local government council. (b) The proposed boundary adjustment is then approved by a simple majority of members of the State House of Assembly for the affected area.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Cross River State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, while about state boundaries, provides insight into the legal complexities and requirements for territorial adjustments at different levels of government.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: In the UK, local government boundary changes are typically managed by independent boundary commissions, which conduct reviews and make recommendations based on criteria like community identity and administrative efficiency, subject to parliamentary approval.

Australian states have legislative power over local government, and boundary adjustments are typically governed by state laws, often involving public consultation and decisions by state ministers or independent commissions.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (5) An Act of the National Assembly passed in accordance with this section shall make consequential provisions with respect to the names and headquarters of State or Local government areas as provided in section 3 of this Constitution and in Parts I and II of the First Schedule to this Constitution.
content.plain_english: Any law passed by the National Assembly under this section (Section 8) must also include follow-up provisions regarding the names and main administrative centers (headquarters) of the new States or local government areas, as specified in Section 3 and Parts I and II of the First Schedule of this Constitution.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of the Federation v. Attorney-General of Lagos State
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, concerning the creation of new local governments, implicitly deals with the administrative and legal implications, including the naming and official recognition of such entities.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 3 (Formation of new States and alteration of areas, boundaries or names of existing States)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: When new states are formed or boundaries altered in India, the law passed by Parliament includes consequential provisions for the names and representation of the affected areas, often amending the First Schedule of the Constitution.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (6) For the purpose of enabling the National Assembly to exercise the powers conferred upon it by subsection (5) of this section, each House of Assembly shall, after the creation of more local government areas pursuant to subsection (3) of this section, make adequate returns to each House of the National Assembly.
content.plain_english: To allow the National Assembly to carry out its responsibilities under subsection (5) (regarding names and headquarters), every State House of Assembly must provide sufficient reports and information to both houses of the National Assembly after creating new local government areas as per subsection (3).

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Lagos State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case highlighted the need for proper communication and adherence to constitutional procedures between state and federal governments regarding local government creation and recognition.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Full Faith and Credit Clause (Article IV, Section 1)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: While not directly comparable, the principle of inter-governmental communication and recognition of official acts is fundamental in federal systems. States are often required to report changes in their internal structures to federal authorities for proper record-keeping and federal interaction.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 9. (1) The National Assembly may, subject to the provision of this section, alter any of the provisions of this Constitution.
content.plain_english: The National Assembly has the power to change any part of this Constitution, but it must follow the specific rules outlined in this section.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Bendel State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case dealt with the procedure for amending the Constitution and the powers of the National Assembly in that regard, affirming the supremacy of the constitutional amendment process.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Constitution also provides a mechanism for its own amendment, requiring a supermajority vote in Congress and ratification by the states, similar to the principle of a special procedure for constitutional changes.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 368 (Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Constitution outlines a detailed procedure for amendment, requiring different majorities depending on the nature of the amendment, reflecting the solemnity of constitutional changes.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (2) An Act of the National Assembly for the alteration of this Constitution, not being an Act to which section 8 of this Constitution applies, shall not be passed in either House of the National Assembly unless the proposal is supported by the votes of not less than two-thirds majority of all the members of that House and approved by resolution of the Houses of Assembly of not less than two-thirds of all the States.
content.plain_english: To change any part of this Constitution (except for matters covered by Section 8, which has its own rules), a bill cannot pass in either the Senate or the House of Representatives unless it receives the support of at least two-thirds of all members in that specific House. Additionally, the proposed change must be approved by a resolution from the Houses of Assembly in at least two-thirds of all the States.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Bendel State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case is a foundational ruling on the procedure for constitutional amendments in Nigeria, emphasizing the strict adherence to the prescribed majorities and state approvals.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Constitution requires a two-thirds vote of both houses of Congress and ratification by three-fourths of the states for most amendments, a similar supermajority and federal structure requirement.

Australia requires a bill to be passed by an absolute majority in both Houses of Parliament and then approved by a majority of voters in a referendum, and a majority of voters in a majority of states, for constitutional amendments.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (3) An Act of the National Assembly for the purpose of altering the provisions of this section, section 8 or Chapter IV of this Constitution shall not be passed by either House of the National Assembly unless the proposal is approved by the votes of not less than four-fifths majority of all the members of each House, and also approved by resolution of the House of Assembly of not less than two-third of all States.
content.plain_english: To change specific parts of the Constitution—namely, this section (Section 9), Section 8 (on creating states and local governments), or Chapter IV (on Fundamental Rights)—a bill cannot pass in either the Senate or the House of Representatives unless it receives the support of at least four-fifths of all members in each House. Additionally, the proposed change must be approved by a resolution from the Houses of Assembly in at least two-thirds of all the States.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Bendel State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case underscores the principle that certain fundamental provisions of the Constitution, like those related to federal structure or fundamental rights, often require a more stringent amendment process.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 368 (Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Constitution requires a special majority (two-thirds of members present and voting, and a majority of the total membership) for most amendments, but for amendments affecting the federal structure or fundamental rights, it also requires ratification by at least half of the state legislatures, similar to Nigeria's tiered approach.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Basic Law (Grundgesetz) - Article 79 (Amendment of the Basic Law)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The German Basic Law requires a two-thirds majority of the members of the Bundestag and two-thirds of the votes of the Bundesrat for amendments. Certain core principles (like human dignity, federal structure) are entrenched and cannot be amended at all (eternity clause).

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (4) For the purposes of section 8 of this Constitution and of subsections (2) and (3) of this section, the number of members of each House of the National Assembly shall, notwithstanding any vacancy, be deemed to be the number of members specified in sections 48 and 49 of this Constitution.
content.plain_english: When calculating the required majorities for Section 8 (creating states/local governments) and subsections (2) and (3) of this section (constitutional amendments), the total number of members in each House of the National Assembly will be considered as the full number specified in Sections 48 and 49 of this Constitution, even if there are vacant seats.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Bendel State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, in its discussion of constitutional amendment procedures, implicitly supports the strict interpretation of 'number of members' to prevent manipulation of majorities, even if not directly addressing vacancies.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: While not explicitly stated in the US Constitution, the interpretation of 'two-thirds of both Houses' for amendments generally refers to two-thirds of a quorum, but for more stringent requirements, it can imply the full membership, ensuring a high bar for constitutional changes.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 368 (Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Article 368 specifies 'a majority of the total membership of that House and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members of that House present and voting,' which clarifies that the 'total membership' is a fixed number, regardless of vacancies, similar to the Nigerian provision.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 10. The Government of the Federation or of a State shall not adopt any religion as State Religion.
content.plain_english: Neither the federal government nor any state government is allowed to declare or establish any particular religion as the official religion of the state.

case_laws.case_title: Adesanya v. President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria
case_laws.relevance_summary: While not directly on state religion, this case and others on secularism and religious freedom affirm the principle of neutrality of the state in religious matters.

case_laws.case_title: Fawehinmi v. Akilu
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases concerning the application of Sharia law in certain states have often raised questions about the secular nature of the Nigerian state, indirectly referencing this constitutional provision.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Constitution's First Amendment prohibits the government from establishing a religion, ensuring a separation of church and state, a principle similar to Nigeria's prohibition of a state religion.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Preamble (Secular) and Articles 25-28 (Right to Freedom of Religion)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India is a secular state, and its constitution guarantees freedom of conscience and the right to freely profess, practice, and propagate religion, while prohibiting state endorsement of any particular religion.
France is a secular republic ('laïque'), meaning the state is neutral towards religion and guarantees freedom of conscience, a strong separation between state and religious institutions.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 11. (1) The National Assembly may make laws for the Federation or any part therefore with respect to the maintenance and securing of public safety and public order and providing, maintaining and securing of such supplies and service as may be designed by the National Assembly as essential supplies and services.
content.plain_english: The National Assembly has the authority to create laws for the entire country or any part of it concerning the protection and upkeep of public safety and order. This also includes making laws for providing, maintaining, and securing any goods and services that the National Assembly identifies as essential.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Ondo State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, concerning the Public Order Act, clarified the legislative competence of the National Assembly and State Houses of Assembly regarding public order, directly relevant to this section.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Commerce Clause (Article I, Section 8, Clause 3) and General Welfare Clause
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Congress has powers to regulate interstate commerce and provide for the general welfare, which are often interpreted to allow federal legislation on public safety, order, and essential services, especially during emergencies or for national infrastructure.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Peace, Order, and Good Government (POGG) clause (Constitution Act, 1867, Section 91)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Canada's federal Parliament has a broad power to legislate for the 'Peace, Order, and Good Government' of Canada, which is used to justify federal laws on matters of national concern, including public safety and essential services.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (2) Nothing in this section shall preclude a House of Assembly from making laws with respect to the matter referred to in this section, including the provision for maintenance and securing of such supplies and services as may be designated by the National Assembly as essential supplies and services.
content.plain_english: This section does not prevent a State's House of Assembly from also making laws related to public safety, public order, and the provision of essential supplies and services, even those designated as essential by the National Assembly.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Ondo State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case is crucial for understanding the concurrent legislative powers of the National Assembly and State Houses of Assembly, particularly in areas like public order, where both levels of government can legislate.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Constitution includes a Concurrent List, allowing both the Union Parliament and State Legislatures to make laws on certain subjects, including aspects of public order and essential services, with federal law prevailing in case of conflict.

Australia's Constitution grants specific powers to the Commonwealth Parliament, with residual powers to the states. Many areas, including aspects of public safety and services, involve concurrent legislative activity, where state laws operate unless overridden by valid federal law.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (3) During any period when the Federation is at war the National Assembly may make such laws for the peace, order and good government of the Federation or any part therefore with respect to matters not included in the Exclusive Legislative List as may appear to it to be necessary or expedient for the defence of the Federation.
content.plain_english: When the country is at war, the National Assembly has the power to create laws for the peace, order, and good governance of Nigeria or any part of it. These laws can even cover subjects not normally on the Exclusive Legislative List (which are usually reserved for the states), if the National Assembly deems them necessary or appropriate for the defense of the country.

case_laws.case_title: Dokubo-Asari v. Federal Republic of Nigeria
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases involving national security and emergency powers, even if not directly 'at war,' highlight the expanded powers of the federal government during periods of national crisis, which is analogous to wartime powers.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Congress has explicit powers to declare war, raise and support armies, and provide for the common defense, which allows for expanded federal legislative authority during wartime, potentially overriding normal divisions of power.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Peace, Order, and Good Government (POGG) clause (Constitution Act, 1867, Section 91)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The POGG clause has been interpreted to grant the federal Parliament emergency powers during wartime or national crises, allowing it to legislate on matters normally within provincial jurisdiction for the national interest.

During a Proclamation of Emergency (which can be due to war or external aggression), the Union Parliament can make laws on any matter in the State List, demonstrating a similar expansion of federal legislative power during national crises.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (4) At any time when any House of Assembly of a State is unable to perform its functions by reason of the situation prevailing in that State, the National Assembly may make such laws for the peace, order and good government of that State with respect to matters on which a House of Assembly may make laws as may appear to the National Assembly to be necessary or expedient until such time as the House of Assembly is able to resume its functions; and any such laws enacted by the National Assembly pursuant to this section shall have effect as if they were laws enacted by the House of Assembly of the State: Provided that nothing in this section shall be construed as conferring on the National Assembly power to remove the Governor or the Deputy Governor of the State from office.
content.plain_english: If a State's House of Assembly cannot perform its duties due to a crisis in that State, the National Assembly can step in and make laws for the peace, order, and good governance of that State. These laws can cover subjects that the State House of Assembly would normally handle, and they will remain in effect until the State House of Assembly can resume its functions. Any such laws made by the National Assembly will be treated as if they were made by the State House of Assembly itself. However, this power does not allow the National Assembly to remove the State Governor or Deputy Governor from office.
keywords: House of Assembly unable to perform functions

case_laws.case_title: In Re: The Attorney-General of the Federation v. The Attorney-General of Abia State & Ors
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, while not directly on this subsection, discusses the limits of federal intervention in state affairs and the constitutional division of powers, which is critical when the National Assembly assumes state legislative functions.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 356 (Provisions in case of failure of constitutional machinery in States)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Article 356 allows the President to assume state functions if the constitutional machinery breaks down, including legislative powers, which can then be exercised by or under the authority of Parliament. This is often referred to as 'President's Rule'.

The German Basic Law allows the federal government to take necessary measures to compel a state to fulfill its federal obligations, including legislative intervention, though it's a measure of last resort and subject to strict conditions.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (5) For the purposes of subsection (4) of this section, a House of Assembly shall not be deemed to be unable to perform its functions so long as the House of Assembly can hold a meeting and transact business.
content.plain_english: Regarding subsection (4), a State's House of Assembly will only be considered unable to perform its duties if it cannot even hold a meeting and conduct its regular business.

case_laws.case_title: Inakoju v. Adeleke
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case dealt with the quorum and ability of a House of Assembly to conduct business, particularly in the context of impeachment, which is highly relevant to determining if a House is 'unable to perform its functions'.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: While not a written constitution, parliamentary democracies like the UK have established rules for quorums and the conduct of business in legislative bodies. The ability to meet and transact business is a fundamental indicator of a legislature's functionality.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 12. (1) No treaty between the Federation and any other country shall have the force of law to the extent to which any such treaty has been enacted into law by the National Assembly.
content.plain_english: An agreement (treaty) between Nigeria and another country will only become legally binding within Nigeria if the National Assembly passes a law to incorporate that treaty into Nigerian law.

case_laws.case_title: Abacha v. Fawehinmi
case_laws.relevance_summary: This landmark case affirmed the dualist approach of Nigeria's legal system regarding international treaties, stating that treaties do not automatically become law without domestication by the National Assembly.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UK operates a dualist system where treaties, once ratified by the executive, do not automatically become part of domestic law. They require an Act of Parliament to be incorporated, similar to Nigeria.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 253 (Legislation for giving effect to international agreements)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Constitution allows Parliament to make any law for implementing any treaty, agreement, or convention with any other country or any decision made at any international conference, association, or other body, indicating a similar domestication requirement.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (2) The National Assembly may make laws for the Federation or any part thereof with respect to matters not included in the he Exclusive Legislative List for the purpose of implementing a treaty.
content.plain_english: The National Assembly has the power to create laws for the entire country or any part of it to put a treaty into effect, even if those matters are not typically on the Exclusive Legislative List (which usually reserves certain powers for the federal government).

case_laws.case_title: Abacha v. Fawehinmi
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case confirms that while treaties need domestication, the National Assembly has the power to legislate on matters to give effect to treaties, even if those matters would ordinarily fall under state jurisdiction.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Supremacy Clause (Article VI, Clause 2) and Treaty Clause (Article II, Section 2, Clause 2)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: In the US, treaties, once ratified, become the supreme law of the land, and Congress can pass laws 'necessary and proper' to carry them into execution, potentially legislating on matters not explicitly federal if required by a treaty.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Distribution of Powers (Sections 91 & 92 of Constitution Act, 1867)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: In Canada, the power to implement treaties is divided according to the subject matter. If a treaty deals with a provincial matter, the provincial legislature must implement it, but the federal government can legislate on matters within its competence to implement treaties.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (3) A bill for an Act of the National Assembly passed pursuant to the provisions of subsection (2) of this section shall not be presented to the President for assent, and shall not be enacted unless it is ratified by a majority of all the House of Assembly in the Federation.
content.plain_english: A bill passed by the National Assembly to implement a treaty (as described in subsection 2) cannot be sent to the President for approval and cannot become law unless it is first approved by a majority of all the State Houses of Assembly across the country.

case_laws.case_title: Abacha v. Fawehinmi
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case reinforces the principle that certain federal legislative actions, especially those touching on matters that might otherwise be state responsibilities (like treaty implementation), require significant state involvement and approval.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: In the US, treaties require the 'advice and consent' of two-thirds of the Senate. While not requiring state legislative approval for domestication, the Senate's role reflects a federal check on treaty power.

In Germany, treaties affecting the political relations of the Federation or relating to matters of federal legislation require the consent or participation of the bodies competent in such a case in the form of a federal law, which can involve the Bundesrat (representing the states).

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 13. It shall be the duty and responsibility of all organs of government, and of all authorities and persons, exercising legislative, executive or judicial powers, to conform to, observe and apply the provisions of this Chapter of this Constitution.
content.plain_english: Every part of the government (legislative, executive, and judicial branches), along with all officials and individuals who hold governmental power, has a duty and responsibility to follow, respect, and put into practice the principles and rules laid out in this Chapter of the Constitution.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Ondo State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, while discussing the justiciability of Chapter II, affirmed that the provisions are fundamental and should guide government actions, even if not directly enforceable in court.

case_laws.case_title: Centre for Social Justice v. President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases challenging government policies based on the principles of Chapter II often refer to this section to argue the government's duty to adhere to these objectives.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Article 37 states that the Directive Principles, though not enforceable by any court, are 'nevertheless fundamental in the governance of the country and it shall be the duty of the State to apply these principles in making laws,' a direct parallel to Nigeria's Section 13.

The Irish Constitution's Directive Principles are for the guidance of the Oireachtas (parliament) and are not cognisable by any court, but they represent a moral and political obligation on the state.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 14. (1) The Federal Republic of Nigeria shall be a State based on the principles of democracy and social justice.
content.plain_english: Nigeria, as a nation, must operate on the core principles of democracy (government by the people) and social justice (fairness and equality for all members of society).

case_laws.case_title: Obi v. INEC
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases challenging electoral processes often invoke the principle of democracy as enshrined in the Constitution, emphasizing fair and credible elections.

case_laws.case_title: Registered Trustees of the National Association of Government Approved Freight Forwarders (NAGAFF) v. The Federal Government of Nigeria
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases on economic rights and fairness in government policy can draw upon the principle of social justice.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Preamble (Sovereign Socialist Secular Democratic Republic) and Articles 38-39 (Directive Principles)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Preamble declares it a 'Democratic Republic' and aims to secure 'Justice, social, economic and political' for all citizens, with Directive Principles guiding the state towards social and economic justice.

South Africa's Constitution establishes a democratic state founded on values including 'human dignity, the achievement of equality and the advancement of human rights and freedoms,' and 'non-racialism and non-sexism,' embodying principles of democracy and social justice.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (2) It is hereby, accordingly, declared that: (a) sovereignty belongs to the people of Nigeria from whom government through this Constitution derives all its powers and authority; (b) the security and welfare of the people shall be the primary purpose of government: and (c) the participation by the people in their government shall be ensured in accordance with the provisions of this Constitution.
content.plain_english: Therefore, it is declared that: (a) The ultimate power and authority in Nigeria rests with the people, and the government gets all its powers from them through this Constitution. (b) The main goal of the government is to protect the safety and well-being of its citizens. (c) The people's involvement in their government must be guaranteed, as outlined in this Constitution.

case_laws.case_title: Abacha v. Fawehinmi
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case affirmed the supremacy of the Constitution and the principle that all government power flows from it, reflecting the sovereignty of the people as expressed through the Constitution.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of the Federation v. Attorney-General of Lagos State
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases on federalism and the distribution of powers often refer to the foundational principle that government exists for the security and welfare of the people.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Preamble ('We the People') and Declaration of Independence
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Constitution's Preamble begins with 'We the People,' signifying popular sovereignty. The Declaration of Independence states that governments derive their just powers from the consent of the governed and are instituted to secure rights, including life and the pursuit of happiness (welfare).

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Preamble ('We, the people of India') and Article 38 (State to secure a social order for the promotion of welfare of the people)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Preamble also emphasizes popular sovereignty. Article 38 explicitly states that the State shall strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting a social order in which justice, social, economic, and political, shall inform all the institutions of national life.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (3) The composition of the Government of the Federation or any of its agencies and the conduct of its affairs shall be carried out in such a manner as to reflect the federal character of Nigeria and the need to promote national unity, and also to command national loyalty, thereby ensuring that there shall be no predominance of persons from a few State or from a few ethnic or other sectional groups in that Government or in any of its agencies.
content.plain_english: The way the federal government and its various departments are formed, and how they operate, must reflect Nigeria's diverse 'federal character.' This means ensuring that appointments and operations promote national unity and loyalty, preventing any single State, ethnic group, or other sectional group from dominating the government or its agencies.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of the Federation v. Attorney-General of Abia State & Ors
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, and others on federalism, often touch upon the principle of federal character in appointments and resource distribution, aiming to ensure equitable representation.

case_laws.case_title: Buhari v. Obasanjo
case_laws.relevance_summary: Electoral cases often highlight the importance of national unity and fair representation in government, which are core aspects of the federal character principle.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 16 (Equality of opportunity in matters of public employment)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: While not explicitly 'federal character,' India's constitution aims for equitable representation and non-discrimination in public employment, with provisions for reservations for backward classes to ensure diverse representation, similar in spirit to preventing predominance.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Section 195 (Basic values and principles governing public administration)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: South Africa's Constitution mandates that public administration must be broadly representative of the South African people, with employment and personnel management practices based on ability, objectivity, fairness, and the need to redress imbalances of the past, reflecting a commitment to diversity and equity.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (4) The composition of the Government of a State, a local government council, or any of the agencies of such Government or council, and the conduct of the affairs of the Government or council or such agencies shall be carried out in such manner as to recognise the diversity of the people within its area of authority and the need to promote a sense of belonging and loyalty among all the people of the Federation.
content.plain_english: The way a State government, a local government council, or their agencies are formed and how they conduct their business must acknowledge the diverse population within their jurisdiction. This is to foster a sense of belonging and loyalty among all people, not just within their immediate area, but across the entire country.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of Lagos State v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, concerning local government creation, implicitly touches on the need for state and local governments to reflect the diversity of their populations and promote inclusivity.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Canada, while not constitutionally mandating 'federal character' at sub-national levels, has a strong policy of multiculturalism that encourages all levels of government to recognize and promote the diversity of its population, fostering inclusion and belonging.

Switzerland, with its linguistic and cultural diversity, ensures representation and recognition of different groups at federal, cantonal, and communal levels, promoting a sense of belonging through federalism and direct democracy.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 15. (1) The motto of the Federal Republic of Nigeria shall be Unity and Faith, Peace and Progress.
content.plain_english: The official motto of Nigeria is 'Unity and Faith, Peace and Progress.'

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Many nations have official mottos, often enshrined in law or tradition, reflecting their core values. The US motto, while different in content, serves a similar symbolic purpose.
India's national motto, derived from an ancient scripture, is a guiding principle for the nation, similar to how Nigeria's motto encapsulates its aspirations.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (2) Accordingly, national integration shall be actively encouraged, whilst discrimination on the grounds of place of origin, sex, religion, status, ethnic or linguistic association or ties shall be prohibited.
content.plain_english: To uphold the national motto, the government must actively promote national unity and cohesion. At the same time, it is forbidden to discriminate against anyone based on where they come from, their gender, religion, social standing, ethnic group, or language.

case_laws.case_title: Medical and Dental Practitioners Disciplinary Tribunal v. Okonkwo
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases on fundamental human rights, particularly the right to freedom from discrimination (Chapter IV), are directly relevant to this directive principle, even if Chapter II is non-justiciable.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of the Federation v. Attorney-General of Lagos State
case_laws.relevance_summary: Disputes over resource allocation or government policies often raise questions of fairness and non-discrimination among different groups or regions.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Fourteenth Amendment (Equal Protection Clause) and Civil Rights Act of 1964
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Constitution guarantees equal protection under the law, and civil rights legislation prohibits discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin, aiming for national integration and equality.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 15 (Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Constitution explicitly prohibits discrimination on various grounds, including religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth, and aims to promote national integration.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (3) For the purpose of promoting national integration, it shall be the duty of the State to: (a) provide adequate facilities for and encourage free mobility of people, goods and services throughtout the Federation. (b) secure full residence rights for every citizen in all parts of the Federation. (c) encourage inter-marriage among persons from different places of origin, or of different religious, ethnic or linguistic association or ties; and (d) promote or encourage the formation of associations that cut across ethnic, linguistic, religious and or other sectional barriers.
content.plain_english: To foster national unity, the State has a duty to: (a) Provide sufficient infrastructure and support the free movement of people, goods, and services across the entire country. (b) Guarantee that every citizen has the right to live anywhere in the country. (c) Promote marriages between people from different regions, religions, ethnic groups, or language backgrounds. (d) Encourage and support the creation of groups and organizations that bring together people from different ethnic, linguistic, religious, or other sectional backgrounds.

case_laws.case_title: Okoro v. The State
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases on freedom of movement and residence, though often under Chapter IV, align with the directive principle of securing full residence rights and free mobility.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (Articles 21, 45, 49, 56)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The EU guarantees free movement of persons, goods, services, and capital among member states, and residence rights for its citizens, explicitly promoting integration across national borders.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 19(1)(d) & (e) (Right to move freely and reside anywhere)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Constitution guarantees all citizens the right to move freely throughout the territory of India and to reside and settle in any part of the territory of India, promoting national unity.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (4) The State shall foster a feeling of belonging and of involvement among the various people of the Federation, to the end that loyalty to the nation shall override sectional loyalties.
content.plain_english: The government must cultivate a sense of belonging and participation among all the diverse groups within Nigeria, so that loyalty to the entire nation becomes more important than loyalty to any particular group or region.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of the Federation v. Attorney-General of Abia State & Ors
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases on federalism and resource control often highlight the tension between national and sectional interests, underscoring the importance of fostering national loyalty.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Constitution's Preamble aims to 'form a more perfect Union,' which implies fostering a strong national identity and loyalty that transcends state or regional affiliations, especially after the Civil War.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms (Section 27 - Multicultural Heritage)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Canada's Charter recognizes its multicultural heritage, and government policies aim to promote a sense of shared Canadian identity while respecting diversity, striving for national loyalty over regional or ethnic ones.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (5) The State shall abolish all corrupt practices and abuse of power.
content.plain_english: The government must eliminate all forms of corruption and misuse of authority.

case_laws.case_title: Federal Republic of Nigeria v. Saraki
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases involving high-profile corruption charges against public officials directly relate to the state's duty to abolish corrupt practices and abuse of power.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of the Federation v. Atiku Abubakar
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, and others on the powers of anti-corruption agencies, underscore the constitutional imperative to combat corruption.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Many countries, including Nigeria, are signatories to UNCAC, which provides a comprehensive framework for preventing and combating corruption, reflecting a global commitment to the principles outlined in this subsection.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Section 195 (Basic values and principles governing public administration)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: South Africa's Constitution mandates a high standard of professional ethics, efficient, economic, and effective use of resources, and accountability in public administration, directly addressing the prevention of corruption and abuse of power.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 16. (1) The State shall, within the context of the ideals and objectives for which provisions are made in this Constitution. (a) harness the resources of the nation and promote national prosperity and an efficient, a dynamic and self-reliant economy; (b) control the national economy in such manner as to secure the maximum welfare, freedom and happiness of every citizen on the basis of social justice and equality of status and opportunity; (c) without prejudice to its right to operate or participate in areas of the economy, other than the major sectors of the economy, manage and operate the major sectors of the economy; (d) without prejudice to the right of any person to participate in areas of the economy within the major sector of the economy, protect the right of every citizen to engage in any economic activities outside the major sectors of the economy.
content.plain_english: In line with the Constitution's goals, the government must: (a) Utilize the country's resources to boost national wealth and create an efficient, active, and self-sufficient economy. (b) Manage the national economy in a way that ensures the greatest well-being, freedom, and happiness for every citizen, based on social fairness and equal standing and opportunities. (c) Have the right to run or be involved in parts of the economy outside the main sectors, and also manage and operate the main sectors of the economy. (d) Protect every citizen's right to participate in economic activities outside the main sectors of the economy, without limiting anyone's right to participate in the major sectors.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney-General of the Federation v. Attorney-General of Abia State & Ors
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, concerning revenue allocation and resource control, directly relates to the state's role in harnessing resources and managing the economy for national prosperity and welfare.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Articles 38, 39 (Directive Principles of State Policy - Economic Justice)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Directive Principles aim to secure a social order for the promotion of welfare of the people, direct policy towards securing adequate means of livelihood, equitable distribution of material resources, and preventing concentration of wealth, similar to Nigeria's economic objectives.
China's constitution outlines a socialist market economy, where the state plays a dominant role in managing the economy and ensuring social welfare, albeit under a different political system.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (2) The State shall direct its policy towards ensuring: (a) the promotion of a planned and balanced economic development; (b) that the material resources of the nation are harnessed and distributed as best as possible to serve the common good; (c) that the economic system is not operated in such a manner as to permit the concentration of wealth or the means of production and exchange in the hands of few individuals or of a group; and (d) that suitable and adequate shelter, suitable and adequate food, reasonable national minimum living wage, old age care and pensions, and unemployment, sick benefits and welfare of the disabled are provided for all citizens.
content.plain_english: The government must guide its policies to ensure: (a) The encouragement of economic growth that is both planned and evenly distributed. (b) The country's resources are used and shared in the best possible way to benefit everyone. (c) The economic system does not allow wealth or the control of production and trade to be concentrated in the hands of a small number of people or groups. (d) All citizens are provided with appropriate housing, sufficient food, a fair national minimum wage, care and pensions for the elderly, and benefits for the unemployed, sick, and disabled.

case_laws.case_title: SERAP v. Federal Republic of Nigeria
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases brought before regional courts concerning socio-economic rights, even if not directly justiciable in Nigerian courts, highlight the state's obligations to provide basic necessities and welfare, as outlined in this section.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Directive Principles explicitly mandate the state to secure a social order for the welfare of the people, ensure equitable distribution of resources, prevent concentration of wealth, and make effective provision for securing the right to work, to education, and to public assistance in cases of unemployment, old age, sickness, and disablement.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Section 26 (Housing), Section 27 (Health care, food, water and social security), Section 28 (Children)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: South Africa's Constitution includes justiciable socio-economic rights, obliging the state to take reasonable legislative and other measures to achieve the progressive realization of these rights, including access to housing, healthcare, food, water, and social security.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (3) A body shall be set up by an Act of the National Assembly which shall have power; (a) to review, from time to time, the ownership and control of business enterprises operating in Nigeria and make recommendations to the President on same: and (b) to administer any law for the regulation of the ownership and control of such enterprises.
content.plain_english: The National Assembly must pass a law to create an official body with the authority to: (a) Regularly examine who owns and controls businesses operating in Nigeria and provide advice to the President on these matters. (b) Enforce any laws that regulate the ownership and control of these businesses.

case_laws.case_title: BPE v. NICON Insurance Plc
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases involving privatization and the regulation of state-owned enterprises or major businesses highlight the government's role in overseeing economic control and ownership, aligning with the purpose of such a body.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and Department of Justice (DOJ) Antitrust Division
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US has agencies like the FTC and DOJ that regulate business ownership, mergers, and control to prevent monopolies and ensure fair competition, similar to the proposed body's function of reviewing and regulating enterprises.

The UK's CMA is a non-ministerial government department responsible for strengthening business competition and preventing anti-competitive practices, including reviewing mergers and market dominance, which aligns with the spirit of regulating ownership and control.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (4) For the purposes of subsection (1) of this section - (a) the reference to the "major sectors of the economy" shall be construed as a reference to such economic activities as may, from time to time, be declared by a resolution of each House of the National Assembly to be managed and operated exclusively by the Government of the Federation, and until a resolution to the contrary is made by the National Assembly, economic activities being operated exclusively by the Government of the Federation on the date immediately preceding the day when this section comes into force, whether directly or through the agencies of a statutory or other corporation or company, shall be deemed to be major sectors of the economy; (b) "economic activities" includes activities directly concerned with the production, distribution and exchange of weather or of goods and services; and (c) "participate" includes the rendering of services and supplying of goods.
content.plain_english: For the purpose of understanding subsection (1) of this section: (a) 'Major sectors of the economy' refers to any economic activities that the National Assembly, through a resolution from both its Houses, declares should be managed and operated solely by the federal government. Until the National Assembly decides otherwise, any economic activities that were exclusively run by the federal government (either directly or through its corporations or companies) just before this section became active will be considered major sectors of the economy. (b) 'Economic activities' covers anything directly involved in producing, distributing, and exchanging goods and services. (c) 'Participate' includes providing services and supplying goods.

case_laws.case_title: BPE v. NICON Insurance Plc
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case involved the privatization of a major public enterprise, which directly relates to the definition and management of 'major sectors of the economy' and the government's role in them.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Nationalisation and Privatisation Acts (e.g., Coal Industry Nationalisation Act 1946, British Telecommunications Act 1981)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UK has a history of defining and redefining 'major sectors' through legislation, nationalizing industries (like coal, railways) and later privatizing them, demonstrating how a legislature can declare certain economic activities as state-controlled or not.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 17. (1) The State social order is founded on ideals of Freedom, Equality and Justice.
content.plain_english: The social structure of Nigeria is built upon the core principles of Freedom, Equality, and Justice.

case_laws.case_title: Ransome-Kuti v. Attorney-General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, and others on fundamental human rights, often invoke the foundational principles of freedom, equality, and justice as the bedrock of the Nigerian legal system.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Preamble ('secure the Blessings of Liberty to ourselves and our Posterity') and Declaration of Independence ('all men are created equal')
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US founding documents emphasize liberty (freedom), equality, and justice as fundamental principles of its social and political order.
India's Preamble explicitly states its commitment to securing justice, liberty, and equality for all its citizens, forming the basis of its social order.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen (1789) and Article 1 of the Constitution ('Liberty, Equality, Fraternity')
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The French Republic's motto and constitutional principles are deeply rooted in the ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity, which are foundational to its social order.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (2) In furtherance of the social order- (a) every citizen shall have equality of rights, obligations and opportunities before the law; (b) the sanctity of the human person shall be recognised and human dignity shall be maintained and enhanced: (c) governmental actions shall be humane; (d) exploitation of human or natural resources in any form whatsoever for reasons, other than the good of the community, shall be prevented; and (e) the independence, impartiality and integrity of courts of law, and easy accessibility thereto shall be secured and maintained.
content.plain_english: To advance this social order: (a) Every citizen must have equal rights, responsibilities, and chances under the law. (b) The sacredness of each individual must be acknowledged, and human dignity must be upheld and improved. (c) Government actions must be compassionate and kind. (d) The misuse of human or natural resources for any reason other than the benefit of the community must be stopped.
