(e) The independence, fairness, and honesty of the courts, along with easy access to them, must be protected and preserved.

case_laws.case_title: Uzoukwu v. Ezeonu II
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases on human rights, particularly the right to dignity and freedom from discrimination (Chapter IV), directly reflect the principles of equality and human dignity outlined here.

case_laws.case_title: Garba v. University of Maiduguri
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case emphasized the importance of fair hearing and due process, which are integral to the independence and impartiality of courts and equality before the law.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UDHR enshrines principles of equality, human dignity, non-discrimination, and access to justice, which are universally recognized and reflected in this subsection.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Section 9 (Equality), Section 10 (Human Dignity), Section 34 (Access to courts)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: South Africa's Constitution explicitly guarantees equality, human dignity, and access to courts, making these principles justiciable and enforceable.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (3) The State shall direct its policy towards ensuring that- (a) all citizens, without discrimination on any group whatsoever, have the opportunity for securing adequate means of livelihood as well as adequate opportunity to secure suitable employment; (b) conditions of work are just and humane, and that there are adequate facilities for leisure and for social, religious and cultural life; (c) the health, safety and welfare of all persons in employment are safeguarded and not endangered or abused; (d) there are adequate medical and health facilities for all persons: (e) there is equal pay for equal work without discrimination on account of sex, or on any other ground whatsoever; (f) children, young persons and the age are protected against any exploitation whatsoever, and against moral and material neglect; (g) provision is made for public assistance in deserving cases or other conditions of need; and (h) the evolution and promotion of family life is encouraged.
content.plain_english: The government must guide its policies to ensure that: (a) All citizens, without any discrimination, have the chance to earn a sufficient living and find suitable employment. (b) Working conditions are fair and humane, with enough opportunities for relaxation and for social, religious, and cultural activities. (c) The health, safety, and well-being of all workers are protected and not put at risk or abused. (d) There are enough medical and health services available for everyone. (e) People receive equal pay for doing the same work, without discrimination based on gender or any other reason. (f) Children, young people, and the elderly are protected from any form of exploitation and from neglect, both moral and material. (g) Public assistance is provided for those who genuinely need it. (h) The development and support of family life are encouraged.
keywords: health, safety and welfare in employment

case_laws.case_title: SERAP v. Federal Republic of Nigeria
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, though before a regional court, highlights the state's obligations regarding socio-economic rights, including access to education, health, and adequate living standards, which are central to this subsection.

international_comparisons.country: International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The ICESCR outlines many of these socio-economic rights, including the right to work, just and favorable conditions of work, social security, adequate standard of living, and health, which are reflected as directive principles in Nigeria.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Sections 26 (Housing), 27 (Health care, food, water and social security), 28 (Children), 29 (Education)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: South Africa's Constitution includes justiciable socio-economic rights, obliging the state to progressively realize these rights, covering many aspects listed here like housing, food, healthcare, social security, and protection of children.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 18. (1) Government shall direct its policy towards ensuring that there are equal and adequate educational opportunities at all levels.
content.plain_english: The government must guide its policies to ensure that everyone has fair and sufficient access to education at every stage, from primary school to university.

case_laws.case_title: SERAP v. Federal Republic of Nigeria
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, before the ECOWAS Court, addressed the right to education as a fundamental human right, aligning with the directive principle for equal and adequate educational opportunities.

international_comparisons.country: International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The ICESCR recognizes the right of everyone to education, with primary education being compulsory and free, and secondary and higher education progressively made free and accessible, reflecting the principles here.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 21A (Right to Education) and Article 45 (Directive Principle)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Constitution makes elementary education a fundamental right and includes a Directive Principle for the state to endeavor to provide early childhood care and education for all children until they complete the age of six years.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (2) Government shall promote science and technology
content.plain_english: The government must actively support and advance the fields of science and technology.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: China's Constitution states that the state promotes the development of natural and social sciences, disseminates scientific and technical knowledge, and commends achievements in scientific research, reflecting a strong commitment to science and technology.
South Korea's Constitution mandates that the State shall establish a science and technology policy and strive to innovate and develop science and technology, reflecting its national priority.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: (3) Government shall strive to eradicate illiteracy; and to this end Government shall as and when practicable provide (a) free, compulsory and universal primary education; (b) free secondary education; (c) free university education; and (d) free adult literacy programme.
content.plain_english: The government must work hard to eliminate illiteracy. To achieve this, whenever possible, the government must provide: (a) Free, mandatory, and accessible primary education for everyone. (b) Free secondary education. (c) Free university education. (d) Free programs to teach adults how to read and write.

case_laws.case_title: SERAP v. Federal Republic of Nigeria
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case argued for the justiciability of the right to education, including free and compulsory primary education, aligning with the state's directive to eradicate illiteracy.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 21A (Right to Education) and Article 45 (Directive Principle)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Constitution makes elementary education a fundamental right and includes a Directive Principle for the state to provide free and compulsory education for all children until they complete the age of fourteen years.

South Africa's Constitution guarantees everyone the right to a basic education, including adult basic education, and to further education, which the state must make progressively available and accessible.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 19. The foreign policy objectives shall be - (a) promotion and protection of the national interest; (b) promotion of African integration and support for African unity; (c) promotion of international co-operation for the consolidation of universal peace and mutual respect among all nations and elimination of discrimination in all its manifestations; (d) respect for international law and treaty obligations as well as the seeking of settlement of international disputes by negotiation, mediation, conciliation, arbitration and adjudication; and (e) promotion of a just world economic order.
content.plain_english: Nigeria's foreign policy aims are to: (a) Advance and safeguard the country's interests. (b) Encourage unity and cooperation among African nations. (c) Promote global collaboration to strengthen worldwide peace, foster mutual respect among all countries, and eliminate all forms of discrimination. (d) Uphold international law and treaty commitments, and seek to resolve international disagreements through peaceful means like negotiation, mediation, conciliation, arbitration, and judicial decisions. (e) Advocate for a fair global economic system.

case_laws.case_title: Abacha v. Fawehinmi
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, concerning the domestication of international human rights treaties, highlights Nigeria's commitment to international law and treaty obligations, a key foreign policy objective.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UN Charter outlines similar objectives for member states, including maintaining international peace and security, developing friendly relations, achieving international cooperation, and promoting human rights, reflecting shared global foreign policy goals.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 51 (Promotion of international peace and security)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Directive Principles mandate the state to promote international peace and security, maintain just and honourable relations between nations, foster respect for international law and treaty obligations, and encourage settlement of international disputes by arbitration, a very close parallel.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 20. The State shall protect and improve the environment and safeguard the water, air and land, forest and wild life of Nigeria.
content.plain_english: The government must protect and enhance the environment, and safeguard Nigeria's water, air, land, forests, and wildlife.

case_laws.case_title: Gbemre v. Shell Petroleum Development Company of Nigeria Ltd.
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, concerning gas flaring and its environmental impact, highlights the judicial interpretation of environmental rights and the state's duty to protect the environment.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: South Africa's Constitution guarantees everyone the right to an environment that is not harmful to their health or well-being, and to have the environment protected for the benefit of present and future generations, through reasonable legislative and other measures.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 48A (Protection and improvement of environment and safeguarding of forests and wild life)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Directive Principles mandate the state to endeavor to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wildlife of the country, a direct parallel to Nigeria's provision.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 21. The State shall - (a) protect, preserve and promote the Nigerian cultures which enhance human dignity and are consistent with the fundamental objectives as provided in this Chapter; and (b) encourage development of technological and scientific studies which enhance cultural values.
content.plain_english: The government must: (a) Protect, maintain, and promote Nigerian cultures that uphold human dignity and align with the core objectives outlined in this Chapter. (b) Support the growth of technological and scientific research that enriches cultural values.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 51A(f) (Fundamental Duties - value and preserve the rich heritage of our composite culture)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Constitution includes a fundamental duty for citizens to value and preserve the rich heritage of its composite culture, and the state also promotes cultural preservation through various policies and institutions.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Section 30 (Language and culture) and Section 31 (Cultural, religious and linguistic communities)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: South Africa's Constitution guarantees everyone the right to use the language and participate in the cultural life of their choice, and recognizes the rights of cultural communities to enjoy their culture, provided it is not inconsistent with the Bill of Rights.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 22. The press, radio, television and other agencies of the mass media shall at all times be free to uphold the fundamental objectives contained in this Chapter and uphold the responsibility and accountability of the Government to the people.
content.plain_english: The media, including newspapers, radio, and television, must always be free to support the core goals outlined in this Chapter and to ensure that the government remains responsible and accountable to the public.

case_laws.case_title: The State v. The Guardian Newspapers Ltd.
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, and others on freedom of expression and the press (Chapter IV), affirm the media's role in holding the government accountable and informing the public, which aligns with this directive principle.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Constitution guarantees freedom of the press, which is crucial for the media to act as a watchdog on government and uphold democratic principles, similar to the role envisioned here.

India's Constitution guarantees freedom of speech and expression, which includes freedom of the press, enabling the media to play its role in upholding constitutional objectives and government accountability.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 23. The national ethics shall be Discipline, Integrity, Dignity of Labour, Social, Justice, Religious Tolerance, Self-reliance and Patriotism.
content.plain_english: The core moral principles for the nation are Discipline, Integrity, valuing hard work, Social Justice, accepting different religions, being self-sufficient, and Patriotism.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Preamble (Justice, Liberty, Equality, Fraternity) and Fundamental Duties (Article 51A)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Preamble outlines core values, and its Fundamental Duties encourage citizens to promote harmony, common brotherhood, and respect for the nation, reflecting similar ethical aspirations.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Section 1 (Founding Provisions) and Section 195 (Basic values and principles governing public administration)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: South Africa's Constitution is founded on values like human dignity, equality, non-racialism, and accountability, which serve as national ethics guiding both citizens and public administration.

hierarchy.chapter_title: Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy

content.legal_text: 24. It shall be the duty of every citizen to - (a) abide by this Constitution, respect its ideals and its institutions, the National Flag, the National Anthem, the National Pledge, and legitimate authorities; (b) help to enhance the power, prestige and good name of Nigeria, defend Nigeria and render such national service as may be required; (c) respect the dignity of other citizens and the rights and legitimate interests of others and live in unity and harmony and in the spirit of common brotherhood; (d) make positive and useful contribution to the advancement, progress and well-being of the community where he resides; (e) render assistance to appropriate and lawful agencies in the maintenance of law and order; and (f) declare his income honestly to appropriate and lawful agencies and pay his tax promptly.
content.plain_english: Every citizen has a duty to: (a) Obey this Constitution, respect its principles and government bodies, the National Flag, National Anthem, National Pledge, and lawful authorities. (b) Help improve Nigeria's strength, reputation, and good name, defend the country, and perform any required national service. (c) Respect the dignity, rights, and legitimate interests of other citizens, and live together in unity, harmony, and a spirit of shared community. (d) Contribute positively and usefully to the development, progress, and welfare of their local community. (e) Assist relevant and lawful agencies in maintaining law and order. (f) Honestly report their income to the proper legal authorities and pay their taxes on time.

case_laws.case_title: Fawehinmi v. Akilu
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases on civic responsibility and the rule of law, even if not directly on fundamental duties, underscore the expectation that citizens will uphold the law and assist state agencies.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's Constitution explicitly lists Fundamental Duties for its citizens, including abiding by the Constitution, promoting harmony, safeguarding public property, and paying taxes, which are very similar to Nigeria's provisions.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Basic Law (Grundgesetz) - Article 12a (Compulsory military service) and Article 14 (Property and inheritance)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: While not a single list, the German Basic Law implies civic duties through various provisions, such as the duty to pay taxes, and historically, compulsory military service, reflecting citizen obligations to the state and community.
